topic: Someone discovers their teenager has a secret social media account
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glow of the laptop screen felt like a physical assault on my eyes. I’d found it tucked deep within a rarely used folder on Leo’s desktop—a password I hadn't even known existed. My breath hitched, a tight, shallow thing in my chest. As I scrolled through the feed, the carefully curated, performative joy of his online persona hit me like a shard of ice. Every perfectly angled selfie, every seemingly effortless interaction, felt like a deliberate construction, a facade built to impress an audience I didn't know existed. My jaw clenched so hard I thought my teeth might crack. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning in protest, and just stared at the screen, a cold, heavy resentment settling in my gut. This wasn't just a secret; it felt like a betrayal of everything we had.

[story 2]

I watched him from the doorway, the silence in the room amplifying the frantic drumming in my own ears. When I finally asked for the password, the way he’d looked up—a flicker of something guarded, something calculating—made my stomach twist. It wasn't an apology; it was a practiced deflection. I clicked the link, and the flood of content was overwhelming. It was a world entirely separate from the messy, complicated reality of our home. A sharp, bitter taste rose in my mouth. I closed the browser abruptly, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet space. I didn't say anything, just turned and walked away, the tension radiating off me like heat. I needed space, needed to process the sheer, bewildering distance he’d created between us.

[story 3]

My hands trembled slightly as I navigated the unfamiliar interface. It was a hidden corner of his digital life, a secret kingdom he’d built in the shadows. As I saw the posts—the curated laughter, the carefully chosen filters—a profound sense of inadequacy washed over me. It wasn't just that he was hiding something; it was the implication that he was living a life entirely disconnected from the expectations I held for him. I felt a deep, simmering frustration bubble up, a pressure behind my sternum that made my shoulders tense up. I finally typed a message, a question laced with a tone that was sharp and undeniably disappointed. I hit send before I could second-guess the venom in my words, the act itself feeling necessary, a release valve for the suffocating feeling building inside me.
